data.title: Wuhan Dazhong Dental Medical Seeks Up to HK$232 Million in Hong Kong IPO
data.description:
Wuhan Dazhong Dental Medical (HKG:2651) launched its initial public offering on the Hong Kong bourse Monday, seeking to raise up to HK$232.4 million.

The dental services company is offering up to 10,861,800 shares, to be priced between HK$20 and HK$21.40 apiece, according to a Monday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The shares are expected to be priced July 7, while allocation results are expected to be disclosed July 8. Shares will start trading on July 9.

Net proceeds from the offering will be used to set up new dental facilities in Wuhan and other cities in Central China, acquire dental institutions, upgrade facilities, boost information technology infrastructure, personnel development, and for working capital.

Haitong International Capital, Haitong International Securities, and Livermore Holdings are the overall sponsors, global coordinators, bookrunners and joint lead managers.

data.title: Gold Hovers Near 1-Month Lows
data.description:
Gold edged down to around $3,260 per ounce on Monday, hovering near its lowest level in a month, as abating Middle East tensions and progress in trade negotiations diminished bullion's appeal as a safe-haven asset.

A fragile ceasefire between Israel and Iran has so far held, reducing fears of a broader regional conflict.

Meanwhile, President Donald Trump announced last week that the US had signed a trade agreement with China and hinted that a "very big" deal with India will follow soon.

Reports also suggested that the US is nearing agreements with Mexico and Vietnam, while talks with Japan and many other countries remain ongoing.

On the economic front, investors are closely watching key US labor market indicators this week, including job openings data, the ADP employment report, and the non-farm payrolls report, which could provide further insights into the Federal Reserve's rate path.

data.title: DFI Retail Group Target Price Raised to US$3.09 From US$3.03 by RHB Research >D01.SG
Chile picks Jeannette Jara to face off against right-wing presidential field
data.description:
Chileans overwhelmingly elected Jeannette Jara, the country's former labor minister, on Sunday to be the incumbent government's candidate and face off against a field of right-wing contenders in November's presidential elections.

Jara, a member of Chile's communist party, won the presidential primaries with 60.31% of the vote while Carolina Toha, the former interior minister and member of the Democratic Socialism party, came in a distant second with 27.91%, with 98.27% of ballots tallied.

In a speech flanked by two of her opponents, Jara criticized right-wing parties for not participating in the primaries and said that her campaign would focus on unity and building a broad coalition.

"I urge you to hold on to each other and not let go, so we can face Chile's far right with the broadest possible front, politically and socailly, and stop it," Jara said. "That's our task ahead."

Only the governing coalition, led by leftist President Gabriel Boric, participated in Sunday's primaries while right-wing candidates, who have led most of the presidential polls, are opting to fight it out on election day on November 16.

"The important thing is that by the end of the day, the progressive sectors are going to be behind a single candidate," Boric told reporters in a press conference after voting in the southern city of Punta Arenas.

Jara, who served as the government's labor minister until this April, gained popularity when she helped pass the government's promise to reduce the work week to 40 hours.

Consecutive re-election is not allowed in Chile and Boric, who rode a wave of left-wing optimism to power following widespread protests against inequality, has seen his poll numbers dwindle since taking office.

Many of his promised progressive reforms, including the drafting of a new constitution, failed to materialize or were heavily moderated by Congress and voters became more concerned over rising crime and immigration.

This elevated a number of right-wing candidates to the top of presidential polls, with Evelyn Matthei and Jose Antonio Kast vying for the top spot.

Matthei, an experienced right-wing candidate, has centered her campaign around "order, progress and hope," while hard-right firebrand Jose Antonio Kast, who lost the election against Boric in 2021, has resurged with a tough-on-crime platform.

If no candidate reaches a majority of votes in November, a runoff election will be held on December 14.

data.title: Hang Seng Tech Index Opens 0.2% Higher at 5351.99
Hang Seng Index Opens Flat at 24274.91
Baht Consolidates; Political Turmoil in Thailand May Weigh — Market Talk
data.description: The baht consolidates against the dollar, but may be weighed by political turmoil in Thailand. Thousands of protesters have recently taken to the streets in Bangkok, MUFG Bank's Lloyd Chan says in a research report. They are "demanding the resignation of Thai Prime Minister Paetongtarn following a leaked phone call with former Cambodian Prime Minister Hun Sen," the senior currency analyst notes. If the protests escalate to levels seen during 2013-2014 political crisis, there could be serious implications for "tourism, the passage of the FY26 budget bill, and economic growth," which would weigh on the baht's outlook, Chan adds. USD/THB is little changed at 32.60. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 9 PM ET: U.S. stock futures rise after S&P 500's latest record high | Japan ...
data.description:
U.S. stock futures rise after S&P 500's latest record high

U.S. stock-market futures rose Sunday, after the S&P 500 and Nasdaq finished last week at record highs.

----

Japan Factory Output Rose in May, But Tariffs Cloud Outlook

Japan's industrial production increased modestly in May, but the rebound is likely to be temporary due to the impact of U.S. tariffs and concerns over a global slowdown.

----

Wall Street Hangs On to Hopes for a Boom in Deals

Blockbuster tie-ups continued in the second quarter in industries relatively insulated from tariffs.

----

Stuck Private-Equity Deals Saddle Investors With Endless Fees

An exit drought has left some $668 billion sitting in funds a decade or more old, racking up costs.

----

Senate Megabill Stuns the Clean Energy Industry With New Tax on Wind and Solar

Elon Musk calls the Senate's latest version of the spending bill 'utterly insane and destructive.'

----

The Unlikely Stocks That Drove the Market to a Record High

The hero behind stocks' rally to a new high on Friday wasn't the Magnificent Seven. It was a discount retail chain.

----

Tech stocks are powering this record-setting rally on Wall Street - but how long can it last?

Whether the record-setting stock rally is built to last may come down to one of the market's most longstanding questions: How broad is the rally?

----

They're in the Top 10% of Earners. They Still Don't Feel Rich.

By many measures, the most affluent Americans are thriving. But $250,000 doesn't mean what they thought it would.

----

A Pioneer in Private Credit Warns the Industry Is Ruining Its Golden Era

Sixth Street's Alan Waxman thinks his publicly traded rivals are turning private credit into a commoditized, lower-returning business.

----

Turning New York Offices Into Homes Finally Works. Now There's a Rent-Freeze Catch.

Mayoral candidate Zohran Mamdani's policies could hurt a promising source of new housing supply.

----

The Stock-Market Rally Is Moving Beyond Big Tech and Investors Are Thrilled

One measure of market breadth recently touched a new high, as financial and industrial names fuel stocks' climb.

----

Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs Data in Focus as Fed Rate Cuts Loom

U.S. nonfarm payrolls figures for June will be closely watched because evidence of a weak job market could increase speculation that the Federal Reserve could cut interest rates soon.

----

The U.S. Economy Pushes Through the Trade War

Employers and investors braced for an economic meltdown. It hasn't happened, though there are headwinds.

----

Trump Says He Has Terminated Trade Talks With Canada

The president blames what he calls an "egregious" digital-services tax on U.S. tech companies.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 9 PM ET: Delta Air Lines Works to Recover After Storms Snarl Weekend Travel | Goldman ...
data.description:
Delta Air Lines Works to Recover After Storms Snarl Weekend Travel

Rough weather struck the carrier's Atlanta hub. Airlines have canceled more than 2,800 U.S. flights since Friday.

----

Goldman Sachs Scraps Plans to Build Hotel Brand in Greece

The firm had big ambitions for three resorts with views of the Aegean Sea. It recently sold the properties, barely breaking even.

----

KKR to Sell Stake in Indian Pharma Company for $1.4 Billion

The private-equity firm has agreed to sell its entire stake in India-listed J.B. Chemicals & Pharmaceuticals to Torrent Pharmaceuticals.

----

Menlo Ventures in Market for Next Set of Funds With AI Focus

The investor in Anthropic and other AI companies is raising about $1.5 billion for new venture funds.

----

Your Next Cruise to Mexico Could Cost More. Here's Why.

A new passenger tax goes into effect on July 1 as the government negotiates with Royal Caribbean and other companies.

----

The Mysterious Billionaire Behind the OnlyFans Porn Empire

Leo Radvinsky is asking as much as $8 billion for the pornography-fueled subscription site he popularized, even as he personally avoids the spotlight.

----

The 33-Year-Old Meat Heir Feeding America's Protein Obsession

JBS overcame scandal to finally secure a listing on the NYSE. Is Wesley Batista Filho ready to be the third generation of family leaders?

----

Saks Secures Financing and Plans to Make Debt Payment Monday

Owner of Saks Fifth Avenue and Neiman Marcus is expected to ask bondholders excluded from the financing to take a haircut.

----

It's Known as 'The List'-and It's a Secret File of AI Geniuses

Only a select few researchers have the skills for the hottest area in tech. Mark Zuckerberg and his rivals want to hire them-even if it takes pay packages of $100 million.

----

Caesars Palace Times Square Submits Casino Proposal

Caesars Entertainment, SL Green Realty and Jay-Z's Roc Nation have been working on the plan, which also includes Live Nation.

----

Couche-Tard's Bid for 7-Eleven Parent Looking Less Likely

Couche-Tard Chief Executive Alex Miller has said he believes the deal can clear regulatory hurdles. But some see scrapping the potential acquisition would allow Couche-Tard to focus more on its operations.

----

SoftBank's Founder Lays Out Vision to Be No. 1 in Artificial Superintelligence

----

----

Sanctioned Steelmaker Finds a Buyer for U.S., Canada Plants

data.title: Reece's Bears Lower Growth Outlook After Update — Market Talk
data.description: Reece's bears at UBS further lower their growth outlook on the uncertain demand facing the plumbing-parts supplier. The investment bank's analysts reiterate their sell rating on the stock, telling clients in a note that they see continued pressure on the stock from macro conditions in both the U.S. and Australia. They cut their fiscal 2026 Ebit forecast by 19% on Reece's latest trading update, pointing to slower U.S. residential construction, housing affordability and increased competition. UBS lowers its target price 24% to A$13.50. Shares are up 0.2% at A$14.15. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2117 ET - China's official manufacturing PMI likely remained in contractionary territory in June, despite a truce in the trade war between the world's two largest economies. The PMI is expected to come in at 49.7 in June, compared with May's 49.5, according to a median forecast in a poll of economists by The Wall Street Journal. That would still be below the 50 mark, which suggests manufacturing activity contracted. China's National Bureau of Statistics is scheduled to release the data on Monday.(singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

2058 ET - The yen weakens slightly against most other G-10 and Asian currencies amid trade-deal hopes. President Trump said late last week that a deal had been signed with China. Commerce Secretary Lutnick and Treasury Secretary Bessent said that the U.S. was closing in numerous other trade deals. "Any news of trade agreements being reached can buoy risk sentiment," four members of CBA's Global Economic & Markets Research say in a note. Such news could "decisively push AUD/JPY above strong resistance at 95.00," they add. USD/JPY edges 0.1% higher to 144.61 and EUR/JPY is up 0.1% at 169.48, while AUD/JPY is steady at 94.48, FactSet data show.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2034 ET - JGB futures fall in the early Tokyo session as investors digest Japanese economic data released earlier. Japan's industrial output grew modestly in May, however, this recovery could be temporary owing to U.S. tariffs' impact and worries over a global slowdown. "All told, we suspect that Japan's economy at best stagnated this quarter," Marcel Thieliant, head of Asia-Pacific at Capital Economics, says in commentary, noting this morning's and other data. Ten-year JGB futures are down 0.19 yen at 138.88 yen. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2012 ET - The Nikkei Stock Average rises 1.2% to 40625.62, tracking Wall Street's advance on Friday. Gains are led by technology- and Internet-related shares, with SoftBank Group climbing 4.9%, Olympus rising 6.9% and Panasonic adding 2.6%. JPMorgan is overweight on Japanese equities, its Global Markets Strategy team says in a recent research report. Domestic equities are likely to garner structural support from two themes--corporate reforms and fund flows, the team adds. USD/JPY is at 144.56, compared with 144.66 in late New York trading on Friday. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1939 ET - Japanese stocks may rise, tracking Wall Street's gains on Friday. Domestic technology shares could advance after the Nasdaq Composite hit a record high. Sentiment may also be buoyed by Commerce Secretary Lutnick and Treasury Secretary Bessent who said that the U.S. was closing in numerous trade deals. Nikkei futures are 335 points higher at 40575 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 144.48, compared with 144.66 in late New York trading on Friday. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 1.4% to close at 40150.79 on Friday, its highest closing level since Dec. 27. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1848 ET - Recent export data from New Zealand adds ballast to Jefferies's bullish view of medical device maker Fisher & Paykel Healthcare. Exports of therapeutic respiration apparatus, denominated in New Zealand dollars, rose by 15% across G7 countries in April and May compared to a year earlier. Shipments to the U.S. were up 16% on year. "NZ exports for April and May imply a robust 1Q of FY 2026 for Fisher & Paykel Healthcare, assuming historic correlations and seasonality hold," analyst Vanessa Thomson says. Jefferies keeps a buy call and NZ$40.90/share price target on Fisher & Paykel Healthcare. The stock is down 0.2% at NZ$36.51 in early trading. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: China's Factory Activity Likely Contracted in June, WSJ Poll Says — Market Talk
data.description: China's official manufacturing PMI likely remained in contractionary territory in June, despite a truce in the trade war between the world's two largest economies. The PMI is expected to come in at 49.7 in June, compared with May's 49.5, according to a median forecast in a poll of economists by The Wall Street Journal. That would still be below the 50 mark, which suggests manufacturing activity contracted. China's National Bureau of Statistics is scheduled to release the data on Monday.(singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

data.title: Japan's Industrial Production Gains 0.5% in May
data.description:
Japan's industrial production gained 0.5% month on month in May, preliminary data from the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry on Monday showed.

On a year-on-year basis, the index decreased 1.8%, worse the April's reading of a 0.5% year-over-year gain.

Based on a survey in June, producers expect June output to increase 0.3% but decline 0.7% in July month on month.

Shipments dropped 2.4% from a year earlier while inventories slipped 3.2% from a year ago.

On a month-on-month basis, shipments gained 2.2% while inventories contracted 1.9%.

data.title: Liontown Resources CFO, COO to Step Down
data.description:
Liontown Resources Chief Financial Officer Jon Latto and Chief Operating Officer Adam Smits will step down from their roles, effective July 14 and the end of August, respectively, according to a Monday filing with the Australian bourse.

The Company's Head of Finance, Graeme Pettit, will succeed Latto as Interim CFO following his resignation, while a formal search for a new CFO is underway, the filing said.

The Company also welcomes Ryan Hair, who will assume the role of COO starting August 11, the filing added.

data.title: Australian Shares Kick Off the Week Higher
data.description:
The S&P/ASX 200 edged up 0.2% to 8,534 on Monday, extending last week’s gains as investor sentiment improved amid easing concerns over broad US tariffs.

President Donald Trump signaled possible flexibility ahead of the July 9 tariff deadline.

However, market participants remain cautious as mixed messages from the administration continue to create uncertainty over which nations are close to finalizing trade deals and which negotiations remain stalled.

On the domestic front, attention is turning to Australian economic indicators due later today, including the TD-MI inflation gauge and reports on housing and private sector credit, which could offer further insight into the health of the local economy.

Among individual stocks, notable gains were recorded by Block, Inc. (3.9%), Macquarie Group Limited (1.8%), and CSL Limited (1.1%).

On the downside, BHP Group Limited and Rio Tinto Limited fell 1.5% and 1.6%, respectively.

data.title: Australian shares head for sixth straight half-yearly rise
data.description:
Australian shares were headed for their sixth straight half-yearly rise on Monday, driven by easing geopolitical and trade tensions and hopes of rate cuts by the local central bank.

The S&P/ASX 200 index rose 0.2% to 8,530.60 points by 0023 GMT. The benchmark was on track to rise 4.5% for the first half of 2025.

Domestic equities recouped from a trough hit in early April as easing U.S.-China trade tensions and an Israel-Iran ceasefire boosted risk appetite.

Gold stocks were poised to soar more than 35% in the first six months of 2025, in what could be its best half-year since 2016's first half, as geopolitical tensions and tariff worries drove safe-haven demand.

However, the sub-index fell 1% on the day as bullion prices eased.

Energy stocks , which fell 0.7%, were highly volatile in the half-year as the Israel-Iran conflict fuelled oil supply concerns. The sub-index was largely unchanged for the half year.

Meanwhile, miners retreated 1.3% after surging more than 2% on Friday.

Further leading the benchmark higher were prospects of rate cuts by the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) as domestic inflation eased.

Swaps implied a 92% probability that the RBA would cut rates by a quarter-point at its upcoming policy meeting on July 8.

Local investors awaited the retail sales data for May due on Wednesday for further clues into rate cuts.

Financials rose 0.7%, with the "Big Four" gaining between 0.3% and 0.8%. The sub-index was on track to jump more than 10% for the half-year.

In company news, Star Entertainment said it received a notice from Hong Kong's Far East Consortium International and Chow Tai Fook Enterprises to terminate the deal to sell its 50% stake in its Queen's Wharf project in Brisbane. However, shares rose 1.7%.

New Zealand's benchmark S&P/NZX 50 index was largely unchanged at 12,580.56 and was set for its worst half-year since the first half of 2022.

data.title: Japanese Stocks Open Higher, Tracking Bullish Sentiment at US Market
data.description:
Japanese equities jumped at open Monday morning, mirroring the US market that was lifted by bullish sentiment in technology companies over the past week.

The Nikkei 225 index rose 400 points to open at 40,550.79.

The rise occurred despite government data released showing slower-than-expected factory output growth in May, which increased 0.5% against a forecast of 3.5%.

The modest output growth, coupled with concerns over new US tariffs, adds to the fragility of Japan's economic recovery.

Investors are also closely watching the progress of a significant U.S. tax-cutting and spending bill in the Senate, which may miss President Donald Trump's July 4 deadline.

data.title: Top Glove Likely to Post Better Sequential Earnings in 4Q — Market Talk
data.description: Top Glove is expected to post strong sequential earnings in its fiscal 4Q, driven by a gradual pickup in glove orders, better plant utilization, and lower input costs, says CIMB Securities analyst Walter Aw Lik Hsin. Margins may also benefit from cost-saving efforts and improved pricing power as U.S. demand recovers, he says in a note. However, Aw cuts the company's FY2025-FY2027 earnings forecasts by 33.8%-45.5% after the 3Q earnings missed expectations. CIMB lowers the stock's target price to MYR0.77 from MYR0.95 and maintains a hold rating. Shares are 0.7% higher at MYR0.73. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)
